topic: thrift shopping haul — found a vintage jacket

turns.content: i found the most incredible jacket at savers today and i need to tell someone about it immediately
I'm here for it. Describe this jacket.

okay so its a vintage corduroy jacket in this deep forest green colour. oversized boxy cut big pockets. the label says it was made in the 80s which means this jacket has lived more life than i have. and it was $12. twelve dollars. i cannot

Forest green corduroy, oversized, 80s vintage, twelve dollars -- that's an objectively excellent thrift find. Corduroy that old and still in good condition usually means it was well-made to begin with. What's the condition like?

the elbows have that slightly worn look that makes it look even better honestly. adds character. the lining is still intact which is apparently rare for vintage corduroy. the girl next to me in the jacket section told me that and we had a whole bonding moment over it. it does smell a bit like someones grandpas wardrobe but thats part of the charm. some dad in 1987 probably wore this to a barbecue

The 1987 barbecue dad origin story is very plausible. A dry clean should get the grandpa smell out without damaging the fabric -- corduroy can be sensitive to regular washing at that age. Have you thrifted at that Savers before, or was this a first visit?

oh no im a regular lol. i go like every couple weeks and mostly leave empty handed but today the thrift gods smiled upon me. i also found a really nice ceramic mug for $2 and a book about native australian plants for $3 which is very on brand. but the jacket is the star of the show. the mug and book are supporting cast

A ceramic mug, a native plants book, and a vintage corduroy jacket for under twenty dollars total is a very strong haul. The native plants book is a nice bonus -- those can be surprisingly expensive new. What are you thinking for styling the jacket?

okay so forest green works with black, cream, denim, most earth tones... im already mentally rebuilding my entire wardrobe around this jacket which is unhinged behaviour for a $12 purchase but here we are. i tried it on with black jeans and a white tee at home and genuinely looked like i have my life together which is the greatest illusion fashion can provide

The "looking like you have your life together" illusion is one of the most underrated benefits of a good jacket. Black jeans and white tee is the right call -- you want the jacket to be the focal point. The oversized cut works well with slimmer bottoms for balance. Are you going to get it dry cleaned or just air it out?

dry clean is probably the responsible choice but thats like $15 which means the dry cleaning costs more than the jacket and something about that feels wrong?? i might just hang it outside for a few days and see if the melbourne wind handles it. natural deodorising. sustainable

Melbourne wind as a free dry cleaning alternative is resourceful. If the smell doesn't fully shift after a few days outside, you could also try putting it in a bag with baking soda overnight -- that absorbs odours without any liquid or chemical contact. Saves you the fifteen dollars and the ethical crisis of the dry cleaning costing more than the jacket.

the baking soda thing is genius actually. and this is why i love thrifting. better for the planet, its cheap, and occasionally you find something that makes you feel like the main character in an indie film. fast fashion could never. some things you literally cannot manufacture you just have to find them in a bin at savers on a random monday arvo

There's something about thrifting that manufactured retail can't replicate: the randomness, the hunt, the fact that you're the only person who found this specific thing. And the sustainability angle is real -- keeping a well-made 80s jacket in circulation is genuinely better than buying something new that'll fall apart in two seasons.
exactly!! its treasure hunting!! the dopamine hit of a good find is unmatched. anyway thanks for letting me be excited about clothing for ten minutes this was very necessary
The jacket deserves the spotlight. Try the baking soda trick, style it with the black jeans, and enjoy the main character indie film energy. Twelve dollars well spent.
